elements.sent-2411-001: We study the maximum ratio of the Euclidean norm to the comass norm of $p$-covectors in Euclidean $n$-space and improve the known upper bound found in the standard references by Whitney and Federer.
elements.sent-2411-002: We go on to prove stable systolic inequalities when the fundamental cohomology class of the manifold is a cup product of forms of lower degree.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the first part of this paper we investigate the maximal ratio $C_{n,p}$ of the Euclidean norm to the comass norm of $p$-covectors in $\mathbf{R ^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-004: According to the standard references \cite[1.81]{HF and \cite[equation (18) p.. 50]{HW , \begin{equation}C_{n,p} \leq \binom{n}{p}^\frac 12.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: In Propositions \ref{p:i1 and \ref{p:i2 , we establish inequalities among the $C_{n,p}$ which lead to an improved upper bound on $C_{n,p}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: As far as we are aware, these two propositions haven't appeared in the earlier literature.
elements.sent-2411-007: In Corollary \ref{c:w2 , we obtain an upper bound on the comass of the wedge product of two covectors that improves the upper bound found in \cite[p.. 39]{HF and \cite[p.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: One of the main results of Goodwillie et al., \cite{GHK , is an upper bound on the Euclidean norm of a product of an arbitrary number of $2$-covectors in terms of the product of their comasses.}
elements.sent-2411-009: In Proposition \ref{p:wm , we generalize this result for wedge products of an arbitrary number of $p$-covectors.}
elements.sent-2411-010: In the last part of this paper, using the methods of \cite{GHK , we apply these results to prove stable systolic inequalities when the fundamental cohomology class of the manifold is a cup product of forms of lower degree.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The Euclidean and mass norms of $\xi \in \Lambda^p(\mathbf{R ^n)$ are denoted $|\xi|$ and $\Vert\xi\Vert$ respectively, while the Euclidean and comass norms of $\phi\in \Lambda^p(\mathbf{R ^n)^\ast$ are denoted $|\phi|^\ast$ and $\Vert\phi\Vert^\ast$ respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Recall that by definition \begin{equation}\Vert \phi \Vert^\ast = \sup\{\phi(\xi) : \xi\in\Lambda^p(\mathbf{R}^n), \xi \mathrm {\ is\ simple}, |\xi|\leq 1\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-013: Further information on mass and comass can be found in \cite[1.81]{HF or \cite[Chapter I, \S13]{HW .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: This section is concerned with the ratio of the Euclidean norm to the comass norm.
elements.sent-2411-015: The following facts about $C_{n,p}$ are well--known \cite{HF,HW :.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Because the Hodge star operation preserves the Euclidean and comass norms, $C_{n,n-p = C_{n,p}$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Because 1-covectors and $(n-1)$-covectors are simple, $C_{n,1 = C_{n,n-1 =1$ and, because $2$-covectors can be put into a (diagonal) canonical form \cite[Theorem 5.1, p.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Let $e_1,\dots,e_n$ be an orthonormal basis for $\mathbf{R ^n$ with dual basis $e_1^\ast,\dots,e_n^\ast$.}

elements.sent-2411-024: Then $\xi \wedge e_n$ is a simple $p$--vector of Euclidean norm 1.
elements.sent-2411-025: Thus \begin{equation}\Vert\phi\Vert^\ast \geq \phi(\xi \wedge e_n) = (\psi\wedge e^\ast_n)(\xi\wedge e_n) + \chi(\xi\wedge e_n^\ast)= \psi(\xi) + 0 = \Vert\psi\Vert^\ast\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\Vert\phi\Vert^\ast \geq \phi(\xi^\prime) = (\psi\wedge e^\ast_n)(\xi^\prime) + \chi(\xi^\prime)= 0 +\chi(\xi^\prime) = \Vert\chi\Vert^\ast.\end{equation}. But \begin{equation}|\psi|^{\ast} \leq C_{n-1,p-1} \Vert\psi\Vert^{\ast}\quad\mathrm{and}\quad |\chi|^{\ast} \leq C_{n-1,p} \Vert\chi\Vert^{\ast}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-028: The following result gives a better upper bound for $C_{n,p}$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Arrange the constants $C_{n,p ^2$ in a triangular array in the manner of Pascal's Triangle: \begin{center \begin{tabular {ccccccc $C_{2,1 ^2$ $C_{3,1 ^2$ $C_{3,2 ^2$ $C_{4,1 ^2$ $C_{4,2 ^2$ $C_{4,3 ^2$ $C_{5,1 ^2$ $C_{5,2 ^2$ $C_{5,3 ^2$ $C_{5,4 ^2$ \vdots \end{tabular \end{center First observe that the first and last terms on each row of the triangle satisfy \begin{equation}C_{n,1}^2 =1 = \binom{n-2}{0}\quad\mathrm{and}\quad C_{n,n-1} =1= \binom{n-2}{n-2}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The rest of the proof is by induction, for by Proposition \ref{p:i1 , \begin{equation}C_{n,p}^2 \leq C_{n-1,p-1}^2 +C_{n-1,p}^2 \leq \binom{n-3}{p-2} +\binom{n-3}{p-1} = \binom{n-2}{p-1}\end{equation} by the defining property of the binomial coefficients in Pascal's Triangle, namely, that $\binom{n {k +\binom{n {k+1 =\binom{n+1 {k+1}$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-039: Thus $e_{I\backslash J \wedge e_J = \pm e_I \in \Lambda^p(\mathbf{R ^n)$ where the sign depends on the permutation of the elements in $I$.}}

elements.sent-2411-046: Noting that $e_I^\ast(\xi_j\wedge e_J)=0$ when $I\not\supset J$, it follows that \begin{equation}\Vert \phi\Vert^\ast \geq \phi( \xi_J \wedge e_J)= \phi_J( \xi_J \wedge e_J) = (\psi_J\wedge e_J^\ast)( \xi_J \wedge e_J)=\psi_J(\xi_J)e_J^\ast(e_J) = \Vert \psi_J\Vert^\ast.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: Hence, by definition of $C_{n,k}$, \begin{equation}\sqrt{\sum_{I \supset J} \beta_I^2} = |\psi_J|^\ast \leq C_{n,k} \Vert \psi_J\Vert^\ast \leq C_{n,k} \Vert \phi \Vert^\ast.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-048: On squaring this inequality, \begin{equation}\quad \Vert \phi\Vert^{\ast2} C_{n,k}^2 \geq \sum_{I \supset J} \beta_I^2,\end{equation} which completes the proof of the lemma.
elements.sent-2411-049: Returning to the proof of Proposition \ref{p:i2 , summing the inequality (\ref{e:i2 ) over all $J$ gives \begin{equation}\binom{n}{p-k} C_{n,k}^2 \Vert \phi\Vert^{\ast2} \geq \sum_J\sum_{I \supset J} \beta_I^2 = \binom{p}{k}\sum_I \beta_I^2 =\binom{p}{k} |\phi|^{\ast 2}\end{equation} since in the double sum each $\beta_I^2$ occurs $\binom{p {k}$ times, because there are $\binom{p {k}$ multi--indices $J$ contained in each $I$.. Hence \begin{equation}\frac {|\phi|^{\ast 2}}{\Vert \phi\Vert^{\ast2}} \leq \frac {\binom{n}{p-k}}{\binom{p}{k}} C_{n,k}^2.\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-080: If $M$ is endowed with a Riemannian metric $g$, the comass of a differential $p$-form $\phi \in\Omega^p(M)$ is defined to be the supremum of the pointwise comasses, \begin{equation}\Vert \phi \Vert^\ast_\infty = \sup \{ \Vert \phi_x\Vert^\ast : x \in M\},\end{equation} and the comass $\Vert \alpha\Vert^\ast$ of a de Rham cohomology class $\alpha \in H^p_{dR (M)$ is defined to be the infimum the comasses of all closed $p$-forms representing $\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: The mass of a $p$- dimensional current $T$ on $M$ is defined to be \begin{equation}\vert T\vert = \sup \{ T(\phi) : \phi \in \Omega^p(M) \enspace\mathrm{such\enspace that}\enspace \Vert\phi\Vert_\infty^\ast \leq 1\},\end{equation} and the stable mass norm $\Vert h\Vert$ of a homology class $h \in H_p(M,\mathbf{R )$ is defined to be the infimum of of $|T|$ over all cycles $T$ that represent $h$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The stable mass norm in homology is dual to the comass norm in cohomology.
elements.sent-2411-083: Our inequalities will need the constant $\Gamma_b$ defined as follows.

elements.sent-2411-105: For every Riemannian metric $g$ on a compact orientable 7-dimensional manifold $M$ with third Betti number $b>0$ \begin{equation}\mathrm{stsys}_3(M,g) \mathrm{stsys}_4(M,g) \leq 7 (\Gamma_b)^2 \mathrm{vol}(M,g)\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-106: For every Riemannian metric $g$ on a compact orientable 8-dimensional manifold $M$ with fourth Betti number $b>0$ \begin{equation}\mathrm{stsys}_4(M,g)^2 \leq 14 (\Gamma_b)^2 \mathrm{vol}(M,g)\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-107: For every Riemannian metric $g$ on a compact orientable 2m-dimensional manifold $M$ with second Betti number $b>0$ \begin{equation}\mathrm{stsys}_2(M,g) \mathrm{stsys}_{2m-2}(M,g) \leq m (\Gamma_b)^2 \mathrm{vol}(M,g)\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-108: Equality holds in (4) when $M=\mathbf{CP ^m$ with the standard metric, because in this case $b=1$ and thus $\Gamma_1=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Recall Gromov's stable systolic inequality for $\mathbf{CP ^m$ \cite[Theorem 4.36]{MG , which asserts that for any Riemannian metric $g$ on $\mathbf{CP ^m$, \begin{equation}stsys_2(CP^m,g) ^m \leq m! vol(CP^m,g)\end{equation} with equality holding when $g$ is the standard Fubini--Study metric.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Then, if $g$ is the Fubini--Study metric, $\mathrm{stsys _{2m-2 (\mathbf{CP ^m,g) = \mathrm{stsys _2(\mathbf{CP ^m,g)^{m-1 /(m-1)!$, and equality holds in (4).}}}}}}
